Software Versioning: What Alpha, Beta, and RC Actually Mean
Quick reference on the version-stage names that show up everywhere — I keep forgetting which is which, so this is for me as much as for you.
"Did you know Bootstrap 5 is in Beta? It's probably hitting RC soon."
"Did you know Vue's already at the last RC before stable?"

So what are Alpha, Beta, and RC (Release Candidate)? These are software lifecycle stages — they signal where the software is in its development and release process. There are usually two broad phases —
. You'll hear development-stage names way more often:
Completed-stage names — RTM, GA, RTW, Stable — show up less in casual talk, so this post focuses on the development phase.
is roughly a "proof of concept" build — a lot less complete than Alpha or Beta. Think of it as a preview.

are both testing builds, but Alpha leans toward internal-developer-only testing, while Beta is publicly accessible. Alpha features are less complete and change more often, so you need broader testing and adjustment. Sometimes Alpha gets shared with partners; for open source projects, Alpha is often shared publicly anyway.
So Beta is the publicly-accessible testing version. Gaming analogy — Alpha is closed beta (CB), Beta is open beta (OB).
Open source has its own twist on this. Take Bootstrap — the official site shows
before stable. When stable ships, the
suffix gets dropped and the version becomes just
. You can see this on Bootstrap's GitHub tags, including their Alpha releases:
incomplete and possibly still subject to API changes
. So don't put Alpha builds into real projects, or you'll get burned when Beta or stable arrives with a different shape.
If you absolutely need a pre-release in your project, prefer at least Beta. If there's an RC, the risk drops further.
is the final stage before stable. After this, the next step is the actual release. Most open source projects ship after RC1 or RC2. Closed source uses the term less often — from Wikipedia:

Closed source software is less likely to use this terminology publicly. Microsoft used the term for Windows 7. Apple calls products at this stage "Golden Master Candidate" (GM Candidate), with the final GM becoming the official release. iOS started using "RC" naming from 14.2 onward.
Cautious open source projects can iterate through a lot of RCs. Vue 3 had
" for stable — that's the official version. "
" tags are typically Alpha, Beta, or RC.

Quick reference for the version-stage names you see everywhere — Pre-alpha, Alpha, Beta, Release Candidate (RC), and the Latest-release / Pre-release tags on GitHub. Covers when it's safe-ish to use each in real projects, and the difference between open source and closed source conventions (Microsoft's RC vs. Apple's "Golden Master").
